title: Midjourney asks federal court to force Disney, Universal, and Warner Bros. to disclose their own AI use
subtitle: The 'unclean hands' motion argues the studios suing Midjourney for copyright infringement are running the same AI workflows internally — and the court should see the receipts.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Midjourney's lawyers filed a motion this week urging Judge John Kronstadt to overturn a mid-June discovery ruling and compel Disney, Universal, and Warner Bros. to hand over internal generative-AI documents — training datasets, model weights, research reports, business plans, and board presentations about AI. The studios sued Midjourney last year alleging its image generator lets users produce likenesses of characters like Bart Simpson and Darth Vader. Midjourney's response is what lawyers call an 'unclean hands' defense: the plaintiff is doing the same thing it accuses the defendant of doing.

The mid-June ruling had allowed the studios to limit disclosure to 'consumer-facing' applications, keeping their internal storyboarding, ideation, and pre-production AI use out of the record. Midjourney's new motion argues that limit hides the exact information that matters — how much of the studios' own creative pipeline now runs on generative AI models trained on copyrighted material. If Kronstadt reverses, the resulting document production could be the most detailed public look at how Hollywood actually uses AI behind the scenes.

This case sits inside a much larger 2026 wave of AI copyright litigation. More than 70 US and international suits are currently active, and the biggest resolved to date is Anthropic's $1.5 billion Bartz settlement. Every new procedural ruling — like this one — sets a template for what plaintiffs and defendants can compel from each other, and studios that sue AI companies without first getting their own house in order are increasingly finding themselves subject to discovery of their internal AI workflows.

A takeaway for learners: 'fair use' in the AI copyright era is being litigated one motion at a time, and the outcomes will shape what data is safe to train on and what disclosures companies owe each other in court. If you are studying law, media, or AI policy, follow these procedural fights closely — they read as small skirmishes but they are where the substantive rules are actually being written.

sources.title: Midjourney Seeks to Reveal Studios' Use of AI in High-Stakes Copyright Battle — Variety
